evidence_quotes.quote: the preservation of digital video games after their commercial shutdown.

paragraphs.text: Hello, I would like to raise an important aspect that I believe deserves inclusion: the preservation of digital video games after their commercial shutdown. Currently, consumers face a growing issue where gamesespecially online or service-based onesare rendered completely inaccessible when companies decide to sunset them. This is not just an economic loss but a cultural one. These games often represent years of creative work, community engagement, and even personal investment. Once shut down, they effectively disappear, with no legal or technical way for players to access them againeven if they paid for them. Personally, I have two examples: Overwatch 1, a game I purchased, is no longer available in any form after the launch of Overwatch 2. This has left me without access to something I paid for and enjoyed. Vindictus, a game I currently play and care about, faces a similar risk. There is no clarity on what will happen to it if the servers are shut down. Years of progress and community building could be lost forever. The #StopKillingGames campaign has brought attention to this issue, advocating for better rights for consumers and preservation standards for digital games. I urge the Commission to consider incorporating provisions that would: Require publishers to allow for preservation or offline access to games after shutdown, especially for those that were paid for; Clarify digital ownership rights for consumers in the context of live service games; Enable legitimate archiving or emulation efforts by libraries, museums, or nonprofit preservation organizations; Protect consumer expectations around access to digital content they paid for. Video games are more than just entertainmentthey are a form of digital culture. The Digital Fairness Act is an opportunity to ensure they are treated with the respect and permanence they deserve. Thank you for considering this perspective.
